tables.title: define Amazon EBS, Amazon EFS, Amazon S3, Amazon Instance Store
tables.context: This table compares the four main storage types in AWS. The context explains that the key differentiators are the storage type (Object, Block, or File), the access method (API-based, attached to one server, or mounted by many), and whether the storage is persistent or temporary (ephemeral). Understanding these distinctions is crucial for selecting the correct storage for a given workload.

ebs.value: Boot volumes, Databases on one server

efs.value: Shared content for multiple Linux servers

ebs.highlightReason: Single attachment is a key exam point - cannot share across instances

efs.highlightReason: Multi-attach capability is the key differentiator for shared storage

instance-store.highlightReason: Data is lost when instance is stopped or terminated - critical for exam

ebs.highlightReason: EBS is AZ-scoped; must snapshot to cross AZs

tables.mnemonic: SBEF: S3 (Object), Block (EBS), EFS (File), Fast/Ephemeral (Instance Store)
examTips: S3 = Object storage for files, images, backups - accessible via HTTP
EBS = Block storage - like a hard drive attached to ONE EC2 instance
EFS = File storage - shared across MULTIPLE Linux instances simultaneously
Instance Store = Ephemeral storage - fastest but data lost on stop/terminate
Exam question with 'shared storage for multiple instances' → EFS
Exam question with 'temporary cache' or 'high performance temp storage' → Instance Store

tables.title: define Security Group, Network ACL, Amazon VPC, Elastic Network Interface
tables.context: This is a classic exam topic. You MUST know the differences. This table provides a direct comparison of the two primary firewall services within a VPC. The context explains that the most critical differences to know for the exam are their level of operation (instance vs. subnet), their statefulness (stateful vs. stateless), and their supported rule types (allow-only vs. allow-and-deny).

sg.highlightReason: Operates at the ENI (network interface) level

nacl.highlightReason: Applies to all instances in the subnet

sg.highlightReason: Remember: Security Groups are 'stateful' - they remember connections

nacl.highlightReason: NACLs are 'stateless' - both inbound and outbound rules required

sg.highlightReason: Cannot explicitly deny - only allow what you need

nacl.highlightReason: Can explicitly deny traffic (e.g., block specific IPs)

nacl.value: Rules are evaluated in numerical order
A Subnet (can apply to multiple instances)

tables.mnemonic: S-SAND: Security Groups are Stateful, NACLs are stateless with Allow+Deny
examTips: Security Group = Instance-level firewall (stateful)
NACL = Subnet-level firewall (stateless) - first line of defense
Need to block specific IP addresses? → Use NACL (Security Groups can't deny)
Stateful = remembers connection; Stateless = each packet checked independently
Default Security Group allows all outbound; Default NACL allows all traffic
Best practice: Defense in depth - use both Security Groups AND NACLs

CLF-C02.task: Task 2.4: Identify components and resources for security

SAA-C03.task: Task 1.2: Design secure workloads and applications

tables.title: define Amazon Inspector, AWS Trusted Advisor, AWS Cloudtrail, AWS Artifact
tables.context: This table compares four key services related to governance, security, and auditing. The preceding context explains that each service answers a different fundamental question: CloudTrail answers 'Who did what?', Inspector answers 'Is my software vulnerable?', Trusted Advisor answers 'Am I following best practices?', and Artifact answers 'Is AWS compliant?'.

question.value: Who did what, when, and where?

question.highlightReason: AWS compliance reports, not your own

tables.mnemonic: CITA: CloudTrail (who?), Inspector (vulnerable?), Trusted Advisor (best practices?), Artifact (compliance?)
examTips: CloudTrail = API auditing - 'Who did what?'
Inspector = Automated vulnerability scanning for EC2 and containers
Trusted Advisor = Real-time guidance on cost, security, performance, fault tolerance
Artifact = Download AWS compliance reports (SOC, PCI, ISO) - FREE
Audit Manager = Create YOUR OWN compliance reports (not Artifact)
Config = Track resource configuration changes over time

CLF-C02.task: Task 2.2: Understand AWS Cloud security, governance, and compliance

SAA-C03.task: Task 1.3: Determine appropriate data security controls

tables.title: define Dedicated Hosts, Reserved Instances, Spot Instances, On-demand Instances
tables.context: This table provides a summary of the core EC2 pricing and tenancy models. The preceding context explains that the key differentiators are the level of commitment, cost per hour, reliability, and whether the underlying hardware is shared (multi-tenant) or exclusive (single-tenant). Each model is suited for a different workload, from flexible development (On-Demand) to interruptible batch jobs (Spot) to compliance-driven needs (Dedicated Hosts).

spot.value: Massive cost savings for interruptible workloads

spot.highlightReason: Biggest discount but with interruption risk

examTips: On-Demand = Pay per hour, no commitment - good for unpredictable workloads
Reserved Instances = 1-3 year commitment, up to 75% discount - steady state workloads
Spot Instances = Up to 90% discount, but can be terminated anytime - batch processing
Dedicated Hosts = Physical server for compliance/licensing (BYOL)
Savings Plans = Flexible discount model across instance types
Never use Spot for critical databases or production workloads that can't be interrupted

tables.title: define Amazon RDS, Amazon Aurora, Amazon DynamoDB
tables.context: This table compares the primary database services on AWS. The context explains that RDS provides managed relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, Oracle), Aurora is AWS's cloud-native relational database with enhanced performance, and DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL database for applications requiring single-digit millisecond latency at any scale.

rds.value: MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, Oracle, MariaDB

aurora.value: **5x faster than MySQL, 3x faster than PostgreSQL**
aurora.highlightReason: Aurora's key selling point - enhanced performance

rds.value: Traditional applications needing managed relational DB

dynamodb.value: Serverless apps, high throughput, unpredictable traffic

examTips: RDS = Managed relational database - AWS handles patching, backups
Aurora = AWS's own cloud-native database, compatible with MySQL/PostgreSQL
DynamoDB = Serverless NoSQL - no server management, scales automatically
Need JOINs, ACID transactions, complex queries? → RDS or Aurora
Need massive scale, low latency, simple key-value access? → DynamoDB
Aurora has better performance than RDS and more read replicas (15 vs 5)

tables.title: AWS Support Plans Comparison
tables.context: This table provides a high-level comparison of the four main AWS Support Plans. The context explains that for the exam, it is crucial to differentiate between the tiers based on their target audience, key features (like access to a Technical Account Manager), and especially their guaranteed response times for critical issues.
tables.mnemonic: BDBE-OR: Basic (free), Developer (dev/test), Business (production), Enterprise On-Ramp (mid-size), Enterprise (mission-critical)
examTips: Basic Support = FREE for all accounts - includes customer service only
Developer Support = For development/testing, not production workloads
Business Support = First tier with 24/7 phone support and full Trusted Advisor
Enterprise On-Ramp Support = Bridge between Business and Enterprise (5,500/month)
Enterprise Support = Includes Technical Account Manager (TAM) - exam favorite!
Business: < 1 hour response for critical; Enterprise: < 15 minutes response
Only Enterprise gets a dedicated TAM

CLF-C02.task: Task 4.3: Identify AWS technical resources and AWS Support options

tables.title: define AWS Region, Available Zone, Edge Location, AWS Outposts
tables.context: This table compares the core components of the AWS Global Infrastructure. The context explains that each component serves a distinct purpose: Regions for geographic presence and data sovereignty, Availability Zones for high availability, Edge Locations for low-latency content delivery, and AWS Outposts for hybrid cloud deployments. A key differentiator is whether you can run primary compute resources like EC2 on them.

az.value: A data center within a Region

edge.value: A small POP in a major city
Web developer speeding up a site
Speed up website for global users

outposts.value: Run AWS in our own data center

examTips: Region = Geographic area with multiple AZs; choose for data sovereignty
Availability Zone = Isolated data center within a Region; use for HA
Edge Location = CDN point of presence for CloudFront; cannot run EC2
Outposts = AWS hardware at your data center for hybrid cloud
High Availability = Use multiple AZs within a Region
Disaster Recovery = Use multiple Regions
Low latency content delivery = Use Edge Locations with CloudFront

CLF-C02.task: Task 3.2: Define the AWS global infrastructure

SAA-C03.task: Task 2.2: Design highly available and/or fault-tolerant architectures

tables.title: define Amazon S3 Standard, Amazon Glacier, Amazon S3 One Zone-IA, Amazon S3 Standard-IA
tables.context: This table summarizes the key Amazon S3 storage classes. The context explains that the choice of tier depends on the data's access pattern and resilience requirements. The main trade-offs are between storage cost, retrieval speed (milliseconds vs. minutes/hours), retrieval fees, and durability (multi-AZ vs. single-AZ).

onezone-ia.value: Infrequent, and I can lose it

glacier.value: Archive, I can wait for it

tables.mnemonic: SO-SO-G: Standard (hot), One Zone (reproducible), Standard-IA (warm), Glacier (cold)
examTips: S3 Standard = Frequently accessed data - highest cost, instant access
S3 Standard-IA = Infrequent access but need millisecond retrieval - backups
S3 One Zone-IA = Lower cost for reproducible data - only 1 AZ
S3 Glacier = Archiving - cheapest storage, but retrieval takes minutes to hours
Need data immediately but access rarely? → Standard-IA
Can tolerate data loss? → One Zone-IA
Long-term archive, can wait? → Glacier

tables.title: GenAI Customization Methods: RAG vs PEFT vs Fine-Tuning
tables.context: This table compares methods to customize foundation model behavior. RAG adds external knowledge without changing the model. PEFT/LoRA fine-tunes efficiently by updating only a small subset of parameters. Full fine-tuning retrains all parameters but is computationally expensive.

peft.highlightReason: Only adapter weights updated (~1-5% of params)

full-ft.highlightReason: Requires GPU clusters for large models

peft.highlightReason: Only save adapter weights, not full model

tables.mnemonic: RPF: RAG (add knowledge), PEFT (efficient tune), Full (complete retrain)
examTips: Need to use company documents with LLM? → RAG/Knowledge Bases (cheapest, fastest)
Need to change how model responds/tasks? → Fine-tuning (PEFT preferred)
Budget-conscious customization? → PEFT/LoRA uses ~90% less compute
Choose order: Prompt Engineering → RAG → PEFT → Full Fine-Tuning (least to most expensive)
Bedrock Custom Models supports LoRA for efficient fine-tuning

tables.title: AWS Global Accelerator vs Amazon CloudFront
tables.context: This table compares two global traffic optimization services. Global Accelerator provides static IP addresses and routes traffic over the AWS global network for TCP/UDP workloads. CloudFront is a CDN that caches HTTP/HTTPS content at edge locations. Understanding when to use each is critical for the exam.

global-accel.highlightReason: Fixed IPs for whitelist/consistent entry points
Routes traffic but does not cache content

cloudfront.highlightReason: Caches content at 400+ edge locations worldwide

global-accel.highlightReason: Users connect to nearest edge, then use AWS private network

cloudfront.highlightReason: Content served from nearest cache location

global-accel.highlightReason: Detects unhealthy endpoints and routes away

tables.mnemonic: GACC: Global Accelerator = TCP/UDP + Static IPs; CloudFront = HTTP/HTTPS + Caching
examTips: Need static IP addresses for your application? → Global Accelerator
TCP/UDP traffic optimization (gaming, IoT) → Global Accelerator
HTTP/HTTPS content delivery with caching → CloudFront
Low-latency gaming or real-time applications → Global Accelerator
Website acceleration with edge caching → CloudFront
Remember: Global Accelerator is the 'express lane' using AWS backbone network

tables.title: Human-in-the-Loop: A2I vs Ground Truth vs Guardrails
tables.context: This table compares AWS services for human oversight in AI/ML workflows. A2I provides human review of ML predictions in production. SageMaker Ground Truth focuses on data labeling for training. Bedrock Guardrails provides automated safety filters without human review.

guardrails.value: Block harmful content, PII filtering, topics

tables.mnemonic: AGG: A2I (review predictions), Ground Truth (label data), Guardrails (auto-filter)
examTips: Need human review of deployed model predictions? → A2I
Need to label training data with humans? → Ground Truth
Need automated content blocking without humans? → Guardrails
AIF-C01 Domain 4 (Responsible AI) = A2I + Guardrails + Clarify
Regulated industry requiring human oversight? → A2I with private workforce
